title: Gravensteen

infobox.value: ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"); lit. 'the Counts' rock') is a [medieval castle](/wiki/Medieval_castle "Medieval castle") in the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). The current castle dates from 1180 and was the residence of the [Counts of Flanders](/wiki/Counts_of_Flanders "Counts of Flanders") until 1353. It was subsequently re-purposed as a court, prison, mint, and even as a [cotton mill](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill"). It was restored over 1893–1903 and is now a museum and a major landmark in the city.

De Cupere, Bea; Van Neer, Wim (2023). ["Faunal Remains from Archaeological Sites Document Human Impact on the Terrestrial and Aquatic Environment: examples from the last thousand years in Belgium"](https://lirias.kuleuven.be/bitstream/20.500.12942/716667/2/De_Cupere_%26_Van_Neer_2023.pdf)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Medieval castle in Ghent, Belgium

Not to be confused with [Gravenstein](/wiki/Gravenstein "Gravenstein"), a type of apple.

The **Gravensteen** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"); lit. 'the Counts' rock') is a [medieval castle](/wiki/Medieval_castle "Medieval castle") in the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). The current castle dates from 1180 and was the residence of the [Counts of Flanders](/wiki/Counts_of_Flanders "Counts of Flanders") until 1353. It was subsequently re-purposed as a court, prison, mint, and even as a [cotton mill](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill"). It was restored over 1893–1903 and is now a museum and a major landmark in the city.

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravensteen&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins")]

The origins of the Gravensteen date to the reign of [Arnulf I](/wiki/Arnulf_I,_Count_of_Flanders "Arnulf I, Count of Flanders") (890–965) in the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders").[[1]](#cite_note-HHG-1) The site, which sat between two branches of the river [Lys](/wiki/Lys_(river) "Lys (river)"), was first fortified around 1000, initially in wood and later in stone. This was soon transformed into a [motte-and-bailey castle](/wiki/Motte-and-bailey_castle "Motte-and-bailey castle") which was burnt down in around 1176.[[1]](#cite_note-HHG-1)

The current castle dates to 1180 and was built by [Philip of Alsace](/wiki/Philip_I,_Count_of_Flanders "Philip I, Count of Flanders") (1143–1191) on the site of the older fortification.[[1]](#cite_note-HHG-1) It may have been inspired by [crusader castles](/wiki/Crusader_castle "Crusader castle") witnessed by Philip during the [Second Crusade](/wiki/Second_Crusade "Second Crusade"). As well as a protective [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel"), the Gravensteen was intended to intimidate the [burghers](/wiki/Burgher_(title) "Burgher (title)") of Ghent who often challenged the counts' authority. It incorporates a large central [donjon](/wiki/Donjon "Donjon"), a residence and various smaller buildings. These are surrounded by a fortified, oval-shaped [enceinte](/wiki/Enceinte "Enceinte") lined with 24 small [échauguettes](/wiki/Bartizan "Bartizan"). It also has a sizeable [moat](/wiki/Moat "Moat"), fed with water from the Lys.

From 1180 until 1353, the Gravensteen was the residence of the [Counts of Flanders](/wiki/Counts_of_Flanders "Counts of Flanders"). The decision to leave was taken by [Louis of Male](/wiki/Louis_II,_Count_of_Flanders "Louis II, Count of Flanders") (1330–1384) who transferred the court to the nearby [Hof ten Walle](/wiki/Prinsenhof_(Ghent) "Prinsenhof (Ghent)").[[1]](#cite_note-HHG-1)

The foot of a wolf was once nailed to the gate and provides the last known [archaeozoological evidence](/wiki/Zooarchaeology "Zooarchaeology") for the wolf in Flanders.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Subsequent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravensteen&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Subsequent history")]

After ceasing to be the residence of the counts of Flanders, the castle entered a decline. It was used as a court and prison until the 18th century. From 1353 to 1491, it was the site of Ghent's mint. Private buildings were constructed on or around the medieval remains. Ghent emerged as a major centre for textile manufacturing during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") in the 19th century, and the Gravensteen was converted into a [cotton mill](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill"). It was at one point scheduled for demolition.

Parts of the castle were bought up gradually by the City of Ghent, which began a major restoration in a [romanticising Gothic style](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") between 1893 and 1907 under the architect Joseph De Waele.[[1]](#cite_note-HHG-1) De Waele was inspired the approach of the French architect [Eugène Viollet-le-Duc](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Viollet-le-Duc "Eugène Viollet-le-Duc") and attempted to restore the castle to its imagined appearance in the 12th century. Many details added during this period, such as the flat roofs and the windows of the eastern outbuilding, are not thought to be historically accurate.

The Gravensteen was the centrepiece of the [Ghent World Fair of 1913](/wiki/Exposition_universelle_et_internationale_(1913) "Exposition universelle et internationale (1913)") during which the city centre was significantly reshaped. It remains open to the public.

Students from [Ghent University](/wiki/Ghent_University "Ghent University") occupied the castle on 16 November 1949 in protest against a new tax against beer. The occupation, referred to popularly as the "Battle of Gravensteen" (*Slag om het Gravensteen*), involved 138 students who seized the castle buildings, lowered the [portcullis](/wiki/Portcullis "Portcullis"), and barricaded the castle gate. A guard on duty at the time was captured and locked in a closet. After raising banners along the castle walls and pelting passing police officers with rotten fruit, they were eventually detained and removed from the castle, though a public outcry of support led to none of the students being prosecuted for their actions. Their campaign against the beer tax was unsuccessful.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)
